summary: In these excerpts from 'Rules for the Direction of our Native Intelligence', René Descartes outlines his foundational epistemological method. He argues that true knowledge requires absolute certainty, which can only be achieved through clear mental intuition and rigorous deduction, starting from the simplest, most self-evident truths and methodically building up to complex conclusions.

principles.statement: All sciences are interconnected and should be studied together.
principles.rationale: Human wisdom remains one and the same regardless of the subject it is applied to. Learning one truth helps discover another, so restricting mental powers to a single specialized science is counterproductive.
principles.source_quote: It must be acknowledged that all the sciences are so closely interconnected that it is much easier to learn them all together than to separate one from the other.

principles.statement: Reject all merely probable cognition and accept only what is perfectly known and indubitable.
principles.rationale: Having doubts or forming false opinions diminishes knowledge. It is better not to study at all than to risk accepting falsehoods by relying on uncertain or merely probable conjectures.
principles.source_quote: So, in accordance with this Rule, we reject all such merely probable cognition and resolve to believe only what is perfectly known and incapable of being doubted ....

principles.statement: Knowledge can only be attained through intuition and deduction.
principles.rationale: These are the only two actions of the intellect that allow us to arrive at knowledge without fear of being mistaken. Intuition provides immediate, indubitable conception, while deduction allows us to infer truths from known principles.
principles.source_quote: Concerning objects proposed for study, we ought to investigate what we can clearly and evidently intuit or deduce with certainty, and not what other people have thought or what we ourselves conjecture. For knowledge can be attained in no other way.

principles.statement: Complex sciences must be deduced from the easiest and most accessible matters.
principles.rationale: Starting with grand or obscure matters leads to confusion. By focusing entirely on the simplest matters, the mind builds the habit of intuiting truth distinctly and clearly.
principles.source_quote: There is, I think, one point above all others which I must stress here, which is that everyone should be firmly convinced that the sciences, however abstruse, are to be deduced only from matters which are easy and highly accessible, and not from those which are grand and obscure ....

frameworks.name: The Method of Order and Arrangement
frameworks.when_to_apply: When investigating the truth of things and attempting to solve complex or obscure problems.
steps: Reduce complicated and obscure propositions step by step to simpler ones.
Identify and intuit the simplest, most 'absolute' natures of all.
Ascend through the same steps to deduce a knowledge of all the rest (the 'relative' natures).
frameworks.source_quote: We shall be following this method exactly if we first reduce complicated and obscure propositions step by step to simpler ones, and then, starting with the intuition of the simplest ones of all, try to ascend through the same steps to a knowledge of all the rest.

frameworks.name: Sufficient Enumeration (Induction)
frameworks.when_to_apply: When a deduction requires a long chain of inferences that cannot be grasped in a single immediate intuition, to ensure no intermediate links are missed.
steps: Survey every single thing relating to the undertaking in a continuous and wholly uninterrupted sweep of thought.
Conduct a thorough and accurate investigation of all points relating to the problem.
Group items into classes if a complete enumeration is impossible, ensuring nothing is inadvertently overlooked.
frameworks.source_quote: In order to make our knowledge complete, every single thing relating to our undertaking must be surveyed in a continuous and wholly uninterrupted sweep of thought, and be included in a sufficient and well-ordered enumeration.

mental_models.name: The Chain of Deduction
mental_models.description: A metaphor for logical inference. A complex deduction is like a long physical chain. You don't need to see the entire chain at once to know the first link connects to the last, provided you can verify that each individual link is securely attached to its neighbor.
mental_models.example: Even if we cannot take in at one glance all the intermediate links on which the connection depends, we can have knowledge of the connection provided we survey the links one after the other, and keep in mind that each link from first to last is attached to its neighbour.

mental_models.name: Mental Intuition as Visual Focus
mental_models.description: Comparing the intellect's focus to human vision. Just as looking at many objects at once results in seeing none distinctly, trying to think of many things simultaneously confuses the mind. Mastery requires fixing the 'mind's eye' on a single, simple point.
mental_models.example: If one tries to look at many objects at one glance, one sees none of them distinctly... craftsmen who engage in delicate operations, and are used to fixing their eyes on a single point, acquire through practice the ability to make perfect distinctions between things, however minute and delicate.

heuristics: It is better never to study at all than to occupy ourselves with objects so difficult we cannot distinguish true from false.
Never think you know something unless you intuit it just as distinctly as the things you know most distinctly of all.
If you come across something your intellect cannot intuit sufficiently well, stop at that point and refrain from examining the rest.
Exercise your native intelligence by first tackling the simplest and least exalted arts, like weaving or number-games, to learn order.

signature_quotes.text: The sciences as a whole are nothing other than human wisdom, which always remains one and the same, however different the subjects to which it is applied, it being no more altered by them than sunlight is by the variety of the things it shines on.
signature_quotes.context: Rule One, arguing against the hyper-specialization of the sciences.

signature_quotes.text: We need a method if we are to investigate the truth of things.
signature_quotes.context: Rule Four, establishing the necessity of a systematic approach to knowledge.

signature_quotes.text: The whole method consists entirely in the ordering and arranging of the objects on which we must concentrate our mind's eye if we are to discover some truth.
signature_quotes.context: Rule Five, summarizing the core mechanism of his epistemological method.

signature_quotes.text: By 'intuition' I do not mean the fluctuating testimony of the senses or the deceptive judgement of the imagination as it botches things together, but the conception of a clear and attentive mind, which is so easy and distinct that there can be no room for doubt about what we are understanding.
signature_quotes.context: Rule Three, defining his technical use of the term 'intuition'.

anti_patterns.description: Mixing probable conjectures with clear and certain truths.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It forces you to draw conclusions that rest on obscure propositions, rendering the entire resulting system of knowledge uncertain and prone to falsity.

anti_patterns.description: Beginning studies by investigating difficult or grand matters.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It overwhelms the intellect. You must start with the simplest, most accessible truths and build up step-by-step, otherwise you waste time and fail to acquire the habit of distinct intuition.

anti_patterns.description: Deducing too swiftly and skipping intermediate steps in a logical chain.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Whenever even the smallest link is overlooked, the chain is immediately broken, and the certainty of the conclusion entirely collapses.
